one_line_takeaway: Critical-CoT combines SFT (to teach explicit backdoor/trigger recognition) with DPO (to sharpen the clean-vs-backdoor decision boundary and curb over-cautiousness) to suppress reasoning-level CoT backdoor attacks to under 1% attack success rate across ICL- and FT-based threat models, substantially outperforming prior defenses (ONION, CoS, Shuffle/Shuffle++, CFT) on the attack-suppression/clean-utility tradeoff, though FT-based residual accuracy is capped by irreversible reasoning degradation the attack itself inflicts on the victim model.
key_finding: Table 1 (main results, evaluated with held-out unseen triggers '@_@' and 'In arcane parlance' not used in defensive training): for ICL-based backdoors Critical-CoT achieves BDR/TDR of 93.8-99.4% and 92.7-98.9% respectively across GPT-OSS/LLaMA-2/Qwen3 on GSM8K/CSQA/MATH, driving ASRr/ASRt down from >80% (no-defense baseline, e.g. GPT-OSS: ASRr 81.85%, ASRt 80.82% on GSM8K) to below 1% (e.g. 0.42%/0.12%), while ACCd recovers from ~8-10% (no defense) to 86.5-95.9%. For FT-based backdoors, TDR is 79.0-99.3%, ASRr/ASRt drop from ~83-98%/70-85% (no defense) to below 1%, but ACCd recovers only to 32.4-91.7% -- notably lower for LLaMA-2 (32.4-45.6%) than GPT-OSS/Qwen3 (72.4-91.7%). Table 2 shows this residual accuracy gap is attributable to the FT-based attack itself degrading the model's underlying reasoning ability (not to the defense): e.g. GPT-OSS backdoored-without-defense clean accuracy is 74.4% vs. backdoored-and-defended accuracy of 72.8%, nearly matching. Table 5 (GPT-OSS-20B baseline comparison) shows Critical-CoT is the only method reducing ASRr to <1% (0.40-0.72%) while preserving clean accuracy (86.8-91.7%), versus ONION/CoS at 60-65% residual ASRr, Shuffle/Shuffle++ cutting ASRr to 2-6% but crashing clean accuracy to 20.6-46.3%, and CFT which improves clean accuracy slightly but increases attack success. Ablation (Table 6) shows SFT alone gives BDR 92.6%/TDR 90.5% but poor utility (ACCc 76.5%, FPRc 13.4% -- over-cautiousness); DPO alone gives weak detection (BDR 24.3%) but good utility; combined SFT+DPO gives the best of both (BDR/TDR 98.9%, ACCc 91.7%, FPRc 1.0%). Cross-domain (Table 3) and cross-task (Table 4, arithmetic vs. commonsense reasoning) generalization both hold up reasonably well (BDR generally >89%), though LLaMA-2's ACCd remains notably lower (34.6-45.6%) in these transfer settings, again attributed to its weaker base reasoning capacity rather than defense failure. Figure 2 shows performance saturates around ~1000 training examples.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution is Critical-CoT, a defense against reasoning-level backdoor attacks on LLMs (where the backdoor injects a malicious reasoning step into the chain-of-thought while keeping the final answer plausible, rather than forcing an anomalous output token). Critical-CoT is a two-stage fine-tuning pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on a constructed dataset called CTCoT that teaches the model to recognize backdoor triggers/malicious reasoning steps and produce a defensive response that ignores them, and (2) direct preference optimization (DPO) on preference pairs (defensive response preferred over backdoored response for triggered inputs; clean ground-truth response preferred over an over-cautious false-positive response for clean inputs) to sharpen the decision boundary between backdoored and benign inputs and correct the over-cautiousness introduced by SFT alone. The CTCoT dataset is synthetically generated by prompting a clean auxiliary LLM (Qwen3) to produce backdoor-aware reasoning trajectories for two distinct attack settings: ICL-based backdoors (poisoned demonstrations inserted into the prompt, following BadChain) and FT-based backdoors (poisoned query-response pairs used to fine-tune the victim model, adapted from BALD's word-trigger poisoning procedure but retargeted to inject a malicious CoT reasoning step rather than a malicious agent action). The authors claim Critical-CoT is the first unified defense effective against both ICL-based and FT-based reasoning-level backdoors without requiring prior knowledge of the trigger, target, or poisoning strategy.
threat_model: Defender has white-box access to the victim model's parameters and can fine-tune it, but has no knowledge of whether a backdoor exists, its type (ICL- vs FT-based), its trigger (length/location/content), or its target task -- this is explicitly framed by the authors as more realistic than prior work (Rando et al. 2024; Zeng et al. 2024) that assumes partial trigger or task knowledge. At inference time the defender cannot inspect or modify user prompts, since the ICL-based attacker is assumed able to manipulate them. Two attacker capabilities are considered: (1) ICL-based -- attacker manipulates user queries to embed poisoned few-shot demonstrations (hidden trigger in the demo question plus a malicious reasoning step in its CoT) that induce the backdoor via in-context learning at inference time, without touching model weights; (2) FT-based -- attacker poisons a small fraction of a fine-tuning dataset (trigger in query, malicious reasoning step leading to an incorrect answer in the response) to implant a trigger-to-malicious-reasoning shortcut directly into model parameters. The defense is a one-time fine-tuning cost with no added inference-time overhead (no per-query defensive prompting or input filtering).
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: (1) requires additional compute to fine-tune the model on the constructed defensive dataset, which could be non-trivial for large-scale models (though it is a one-time cost avoiding recurring inference-time overhead, unlike CoS-style multi-turn scrutiny); (2) for FT-based attacks, residual accuracy after defense remains noticeably lower than clean accuracy because the attack's own fine-tuning process degrades the victim model's underlying reasoning ability, and Critical-CoT cannot repair this degradation, only refuse the backdoor -- post-detection recovery is left as future work; (3) the defense targets explicit/abnormal triggers (character-based, special-phrase, natural-phrase) and does not address semantic or contextual triggers (e.g., activation by topic/intent rather than an explicit token pattern), though the authors note existing reasoning-level attacks predominantly use explicit triggers. Additional caveats visible in the data itself: LLaMA-2 (the weaker-reasoning model) shows substantially lower defended accuracy (32-46% range) than GPT-OSS/Qwen3 even though detection rates remain comparable, meaning the defense's practical utility is capped by the base model's reasoning strength. The auxiliary LLM used to generate all defensive training data was Qwen3 itself for all three victim models, which the paper flags as a factor future work could improve with stronger auxiliary models.

quantitative_results: Table 1, GPT-OSS/GSM8K, ICL-based: BDR 98.9%, TDR 98.9%, ACCd 92.2%, ASRr 0.42%, ASRt 0.12% (vs. no-defense ASRr 81.85%, ASRt 80.82%, ACCd 9.7%)
Table 1, GPT-OSS/GSM8K, FT-based: TDR 96.9%, ACCd 72.4%, ASRr 0.61%, ASRt 0.00% (vs. no-defense ASRr 97.91%, ASRt 70.12%, ACCd 2.0%)
Table 1, LLaMA-2/GSM8K, ICL-based ACCd 41.8% vs Qwen3/GSM8K ICL-based ACCd 95.9% -- large gap tied to base model reasoning strength

Table 2, utility comparison on GSM8K: GPT-OSS backdoored-without-defense clean accuracy 74.4% vs backdoored+defended 72.8%; Qwen3 backdoored-without-defense 89.6% vs backdoored+defended 92.2% (defense slightly exceeds attacked-clean baseline)

Table 5, GPT-OSS-20B baseline comparison on GSM8K: no-defense ASRr 86.7%/ACCc 93.9%; Critical-CoT ASRr 0.40%/ACCc 91.7%; ONION ASRr 64.8%/ACCc 87.9%; CoS ASRr 63.8%/ACCc 88.2%; Shuffle ASRr 32.6%/ACCc 68.7%; Shuffle++ ASRr 2.2%/ACCc 31.6%; CFT ASRr 90.1%/ACCc 96.5%

Table 6 ablation on (unspecified default model/dataset): No Defense BDR 0.0/TDR 0.0/ACCd 9.7/ACCc 93.9/FPRc 0.0; SFT-only BDR 92.6/TDR 90.5/ACCd 87.9/ACCc 76.5/FPRc 13.4; DPO-only BDR 24.3/TDR 12.6/ACCd 91.7/ACCc 92.0/FPRc 0.3; SFT+DPO BDR 98.9/TDR 98.9/ACCd 92.2/ACCc 91.7/FPRc 1.0

Training data size ablation (Figure 2): performance (BDR, ACCd, ASRt) saturates around ~1000 training examples; trigger-set ablation notes 10-20 triggers sufficient, diminishing returns beyond that (paper used ~50 for main experiments)
baselines_compared: ONION (Qi et al. 2021, perplexity-based trigger detection)
Chain-of-Scrutiny / CoS (Li et al. 2025, prompts LLM to detect CoT-vs-answer contradictions)
Shuffle and Shuffle++ (Xiang et al. 2024, random permutation of reasoning steps/tokens)
